name: Data governance and privacy approach
lever_classification: governance — how data access and privacy are managed
consequences: Establishes a privacy-preserving data ecosystem that supports collaboration while protecting participant identity. Downstream, the process may slow data-sharing workflows due to reviews and compliance checks, increasing administrative burden and potentially delaying analyses.
options: Adopt a tiered data access plan that de-identifies physiological data for external researchers under strict data-use agreements, while keeping raw video strictly off-limits.
Implement on-site encrypted storage with restricted off-site backups and formal data-sharing requests reviewed by a privacy committee before any transfer.
Use differential privacy and controlled synthetic data releases to enable broad methodological exploration without exposing identifiable patterns.
review: Core tension: balancing openness with privacy; a weakness is that the options may underestimate the administrative and legal review timelines and cross-border data-transfer complexities.

name: Recruitment and participant diversity strategies
lever_classification: execution — how to recruit and retain participants
consequences: Broader enrollment increases generalizability and statistical power, but demands additional staff, more complex scheduling, and higher administrative costs.
options: Expand recruitment through regional sleep clinics and multilingual consent materials to improve inclusivity while maintaining rigorous screening.
Offer flexible scheduling, transportation subsidies, and remote pre-screening to reduce participation barriers and optimize site load.
Establish a matched control cohort with parallel recruitment channels to ensure robust baseline comparisons across demographically diverse groups.
review: Core tension: speed of enrollment versus representativeness; weakness is that even expansive channels may still miss certain populations without targeted community engagement and ongoing cultural adaptation.

name: Sleep scoring and annotation workflow
lever_classification: methods — how data are collected and scored
consequences: Improved reliability and throughput; however, personnel costs rise and scheduling complexity increases. Downstream, higher accuracy supports stronger phenotyping but may constrain rapid iteration cycles and require ongoing training.
options: Implement double independent scoring with a formal adjudication process to raise inter-rater reliability and minimize systematic bias.
Scale semi-automated event triage using rule-based heuristics to prioritize human review while preserving clinical caution.
Expand the scoring pool by hiring additional trained raters and instituting shift coverage to ensure timely annotation without fatigue.
review: Core tension: reliability versus throughput; a weakness is that double scoring may still allow drift in criteria over time without regular refresher training and calibration sessions.

name: Facility design, safety, and scalability
lever_classification: execution — how to scale the physical footprint and safety protocols
consequences: Modularity supports growth with controlled risk, preserving safety and comfort; trade-offs include higher upfront architectural complexity and ongoing maintenance costs.
options: Design modular suite layouts that preserve domestic feel while enabling rapid expansion to 12 suites if pilot data meet predefined quality benchmarks.
Institute a continuous safety engineering cycle with monthly audits and adjustable alarm protocols to maintain patient safety without compromising comfort.
Establish a phased expansion plan tied to data-quality metrics and staffing capacity, deferring full scale until pilot demonstrates sustainable throughput.
review: Core tension: growth timing versus quality and risk; a weakness is that expansion triggers rely on pilot metrics that may be noisy or misaligned with longer-term outcomes.

name: Funding governance and industry partnership strategy
lever_classification: governance — how funding and partnerships are managed
consequences: Better alignment of resources with milestones; may slow scaling if milestones prove overly conservative, but reduces risk of misallocation. Downstream, it creates a clear pathway for external involvement without compromising publication rights.
options: Adopt milestone-based funding tranches aligned with recruitment and data-quality milestones to preserve financial discipline.
Create a dedicated contingency fund and a transparent reallocation protocol to absorb regulatory delays and recruitment slippage.
Pursue non-dilutive grants first and only engage industry partnerships after peer-reviewed results to protect publication rights.
review: Core tension: balancing external funding with academic independence; a weakness is the lack of explicit terms on IP and publication windows with industry partners.

name: Data sharing and reproducibility framework
lever_classification: dissemination — how data and methods are shared
consequences: Increases transparency and reproducibility; however, it adds ongoing training burdens and governance overhead. Downstream, broad sharing can accelerate scientific impact but requires careful privacy guarding and sustained operational effort.
options: Publish de-identified raw and derived datasets in recognized repositories under clear data-use agreements to foster transparency.
Develop a standardized EEG-BIDS compliant pipeline and provide open-source processing scripts to ensure reproducibility across centers.
Institute periodic data-handling training and a governance workshop series to harmonize annotation quality and prevent drift.
review: Core tension: openness versus privacy; a weakness is that de-identification may not fully mitigate re-identification risk when combined with rich sensor data.

name: Sensor tiering and escalation protocol
lever_classification: methodology — which data collection approach to use
consequences: Establishes a baseline monitoring approach using low-burden sensors as the default, reducing participant burden and throughput. Should trajectories show insufficient event capture, escalation toward enhanced PSG nights can improve diagnostic yield but increases staffing and data-processing load, with a trade-off in participant comfort and cost.
options: Standardize nightly dry-electrode EEG headbands with contact-free sensors as baseline, and trigger full PSG only when algorithm flags high-probability events for confirmatory scoring.
Adopt a fixed alternating schedule where every third night uses enhanced PSG to balance depth of data with participant throughput and staffing expenditure.
Pursue continuous full PSG across all nights for all participants to maximize data resolution at the expense of higher costs, lengthier scoring, and greater participant burden.
review: Core tension is between data richness and participant burden; the options miss the risk that escalation triggers may systematically miss rare events, leading to sampling bias if the escalation thresholds are not carefully calibrated.

name: Recruitment pacing and stay-extension strategy
lever_classification: governance — recruitment pacing and stay-extension criteria
consequences: Defines enrollment tempo and stay durations, directly affecting project timeline and budget stability. If paced too slowly, milestones may slip and external reviews could be delayed; if accelerated, there is a higher risk of underpowered subsets or increased safety concerns due to longer stays without sufficient interim data quality checks.
options: Institute strict enrollment thresholds with predefined stay limits and early exit criteria, enabling predictable throughput and faster external review cycles.
Adopt flexible admission windows tied to interim data quality metrics, allowing longer stays for non-productive admissions if safety thresholds remain satisfied.
Implement adaptive recruitment pacing guided by interim event yield, with a quarterly governance review to recalibrate inclusion criteria and compensation scales.
review: Core tension centers on speed of enrollment versus safety and data quality; a weakness is that the options do not address regulatory review delays or partner skepticism that could stall recruitment despite favorable yields.

name: Event-triage tool design and validation workflow
lever_classification: methodology — event detection and adjudication workflow
consequences: Shapes how events are prioritized for review; automation can reduce manual workload and speed scoring, but miscalibration risks mislabeling episodes and biasing results, requiring careful validation and ongoing calibration. This lever also impacts data provenance by clarifying when adjudication is human-backed vs automated.
options: Develop a rule-based event triage system using explicit clinical criteria, with periodic human review to calibrate thresholds and maintain interpretation consistency.
Pilot a machine-assisted triage using validated features from sensor streams, with dual human adjudication on flagged events to measure agreement and avoid over-reliance on automation.
Delay automation until multi-center external data are available, and implement manual adjudication-only workflow with standardized scoring rubrics to control false positives.
review: Core tension is between automation efficiency and human interpretive accuracy; a weakness is that none of the options specify how to resolve discordant adjudications or how inter-rater reliability will be tracked over time.

name: Privacy-preserving access and data-sharing governance
lever_classification: dissemination — data access and sharing policy
consequences: Sets the rules for external data access while preserving participant privacy; improves reproducibility but may slow external validation and require comprehensive legal oversight. The approach chosen will influence collaboration velocity and post-hoc data reuse potential.
options: Publish de-identified sensor datasets with restricted video components under formal data use agreements to enable external validation while preserving participant privacy.
Create tiered dataset releases with graduated access for researchers, enabling rapid preprints while maintaining controlled release of raw streaming signals and video segments.
Keep all data local-on-premises with periodic secure transfers to institutional repositories, prioritizing compliance reviews and minimizing public exposure of sensitive video data.
review: Core tension is balancing open science with participant privacy; a weakness is that none of the options elaborate on re-identification risk controls or cross-border data transfer compliance specifics.

name: Staffing model and safety coverage strategy
consequences: Dictates nightly coverage quality and incident responsiveness, directly affecting safety and data integrity while shaping burnout risk and total personnel costs. Misalignment can create gaps during critical events or inflate long-term expenses.
options: Implement rotating night-shift teams with cross-trained technicians and on-call safety support to ensure stable coverage while reducing burnout risk.
Contract external clinical monitors during peak event windows to augment in-house staff, ensuring rapid response without permanently expanding core team size.
Adopt a staged staffing plan tied to enrollment milestones, progressively increasing headcount as data throughput justifies longer stays.
review: Core tension is cost containment versus robust safety coverage; a weakness is that the options do not define minimum response times or safety drill cadences, which could compromise incident handling.

name: Facility design iteration and safety compliance strategy
lever_classification: operations — facility design, safety, and scalability
consequences: Guides ongoing safety enhancements and regulatory readiness; iterative improvements reduce risk but may delay expansion and temporarily constrain capacity, impacting recruitment and data throughput. Budgeting for iterative upgrades must be balanced with occupancy timelines.
options: Pilot modular safety retrofits with feedback loops from nightly audits, enabling rapid adjustments to padded corridors and door alarms while maintaining domestic ambience.
Temporarily limit active suites to a smaller footprint to ensure rigorous safety checks and higher data quality before scaling to the full 12-suite capacity.
Partner with a dedicated safety engineering firm to conduct risk assessments and implement remediation plans on a rolling schedule aligned to ongoing occupancy.
review: Core tension is safety improvements versus pace of expansion; a weakness is that none of the options quantify budget triggers or outline contingency plans should renovations overrun timelines.

name: Enrollment cadence and participant retention strategy
lever_classification: execution — how to pace enrollment and retain participants
consequences: Regulating enrollment pace stabilizes data collection and helps align study milestones with staffing capacity. It also increases the risk of a longer recruitment phase and potential selection bias if incentives disproportionately attract particular participant profiles. Additionally, staggered starts may complicate uniform data analyses across cohorts.
options: Adopt a structured rolling enrollment with clearly defined interim milestones, proactive engagement activities, and participant incentives that align with stay duration while monitoring dropout causes in real time
Implement adaptive recruitment blocks guided by early event yield analyses, pausing non-productive outreach when yield per week declines
Pilot a matched-control enrollment protocol that synchronizes participant timelines with control cohorts to stabilize data collection across staggered starts
review: Core tension: balancing rapid enrollment with representative sampling and participant welfare. Weakness: none of the options explicitly address targeted outreach to underrepresented groups or monitoring consent withdrawal dynamics.

name: Data access tiering and privacy risk management
lever_classification: governance — who sets data access policies and privacy controls
consequences: Establishing access controls and privacy risk management reduces re-identification risk and ensures compliance with ethical approvals in a sensitive data environment. The trade-off is increased administrative overhead and potential delays for external researchers seeking data. It may also constrain exploratory analyses if too restrictive.
options: Institute role-based access with minimum-necessary permissions and quarterly privacy audits across all physiological data streams, ensuring no access to restricted video feeds except under approved research use
Define a controlled-access data enclave where de-identified sensor data are made available to approved researchers under a standard data use agreement and ethics review
Implement a consent-driven data sharing model that dynamically limits access based on participant consent status and ongoing ethics approvals
review: Core tension: maximizing scientific value from rich data while protecting participant privacy. Weakness: the options do not specify concrete safeguards for re-identification risk or evolving consent frameworks tied to long-term data reuse.

name: Flexible facility operations and safety protocol adaptability
lever_classification: operations — how to allocate space and adapt safety protocols
consequences: Modular operations enable rapid reconfiguration of space to match study phase demands while preserving safety margins. The trade-off is potential inefficiencies from frequent reconfiguration and the risk that safety drills disrupt night schedules. It also requires robust staff training to prevent errors during transitions.
options: Adopt a modular room allocation framework that reconfigures sleep suites for high-activity weeks and quiet observation periods while maintaining safety reserves
Maintain fixed suite allocations with cross-trained staff to ensure safe coverage while preserving domestic feel and minimizing staggering disruptions
Schedule proactive safety drills, incident simulations, and buffer nights to validate response times before escalations, balancing participant experience and safety readiness
review: Core tension: safety and reliability versus operational efficiency and participant comfort. Weakness: options do not explicitly tie reconfiguration to regulatory approvals or cost implications of frequent changes.

name: Annotation throughput optimization and scorer calibration
lever_classification: methodology — how to score events and calibrate raters
consequences: Improving annotation throughput increases data yield and reduces lag between night collection and scoring. The trade-off is potential reviewer fatigue and risk of calibration drift if training is infrequent. It may also shift focus from data quality to speed unless carefully managed.
options: Institutionalize weekly scorer calibration sessions using a blinded set of annotated nights to maintain high inter-rater agreement and reduce adjudication time
Implement semi-automated event tagging to triage nights for human scoring while preserving manual review for ambiguous cases
Create a second independent scoring pass for all events with rapid adjudication meetings to resolve discrepancies promptly
review: Core tension: maintaining high scoring reliability while increasing throughput. Weakness: options do not address sustained training cycles or long-term inter-rater reliability metrics beyond initial calibration.

name: Funding strategy and milestone-driven governance
lever_classification: governance — how funding is obtained and governed
consequences: A phased funding approach tied to enrollment and data quality milestones improves financial predictability and reduces risk of overrun. The trade-off is potential incentive pressure to meet milestones at the expense of data depth or quality investigations if timelines tighten. It may also complicate long-range planning without committed multi-year commitments.
options: Adopt a phase-gate funding plan tied to enrollment milestones and data quality metrics with quarterly reviews by the external advisory board
Pursue a foundational core grant blended with early publication targets to attract later-stage industry partnerships while safeguarding publication rights
Diversify funding across national sources and philanthropic grants to reduce reliance on a single source, ensuring flexibility in project scope and timelines
review: Core tension: stable funding versus academic independence and timely outputs. Weakness: options may understate the need for explicit conflict-of-interest controls and clear publication-scope boundaries with potential funders.

name: Dissemination framework alignment with Aim 2 phenotyping
lever_classification: dissemination — how findings are shared with the scientific community
consequences: A structured dissemination plan helps translate longitudinal phenotyping into publishable insights while respecting privacy constraints. The trade-off is potential slower public sharing of full methodological details and restricted data disclosure that could limit external validation. It requires clear data descriptors and governance in every outlet.
options: Publish method-validation results and longitudinal phenotyping in open-access journals, while restricting raw video data to secure repositories under access controls
Develop a standardized data descriptor, metadata schema, and analysis pipelines to enable reproducibility without exposing sensitive participant content
Coordinate with the university's communications office to translate findings for clinical practice while maintaining rigorous peer-review and data governance
review: Core tension: broad dissemination versus stringent data privacy. Weakness: options may not specify strategic sequencing of findings across journals and venues to maximize impact within governance constraints.
